title: Erythema Nodosum
oneLiner: Acute, tender, red nodular panniculitis on shins; usually reactive to infection, sarcoidosis, drugs, or IBD — rarely dangerous itself, but the trigger can be.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Necrotising fasciitis** (if unilateral or rapidly spreading, severe pain out of proportion, crepitus/bullae, systemic toxicity) — this is NOT erythema nodosum. If in doubt, call surgical registrar immediately.
- **Sepsis from underlying infection** (streptococcal pharyngitis, Yersinia, TB) — high fever, hypotension, raised lactate, focus on throat/lungs/gut.
- **Pulmonary embolism** (if unilateral leg swelling with pleuritic chest pain or hypoxia) — EN is bilateral; a single swollen leg should prompt DVT exclusion.
sections.Presentation:
- Tender, erythematous, warm nodules: 1–5 cm diameter, typically bilateral on **anterior shins** (rarely thighs/arms)
- Nodules do **not** ulcerate, do **not** suppurate — they resolve over 3–6 weeks, leaving bruise-like discolouration
- Often preceded by:
  - Fever, malaise, arthralgia (ankles, knees)
  - Recent sore throat, cough, GI upset, or new medication
- **Key exam findings:** nodules are deep (panniculitis), non-mobile, exquisitely tender; overlying skin may be shiny; no ulceration; bilateral ± symmetrical
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Cellulitis / erysipelas** — unilateral, well-demarcated erythema, no discrete nodules; systemic upset out of proportion
- **Deep vein thrombosis** — unilateral swelling, calf tenderness, no nodules; Doppler USS needed
- **Vasculitis** (e.g. polyarteritis nodosa) — ulceration, livedo reticularis, multiorgan symptoms, palpable purpura
- **Insect bites** — asymmetrical, pruritic, central punctum
- **Pancreatic panniculitis** — nodules on legs + abdomen, occurs in acute pancreatitis (elevated lipase)
- **Lupus profundus** — more chronic, indurated plaques, associated SLE features
- **Splitter:** Bilateral shins → EN until proven otherwise; unilateral → rule out DVT/cellulitis first
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Temperature, heart rate, BP, respiratory rate, oxygen saturations
  - Throat inspection + palpation (exudate, cervical lymphadenopathy)
  - Examine chest (lymphadenopathy? sarcoidosis clue)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, ESR
  - Blood cultures (if febrile)
  - ASOT + anti-DNAse B (if suspected Group A Strep)
  - Stool cultures + Yersinia serology (if GI prodrome)
  - Serum ACE (sarcoidosis — not diagnostic alone but supportive)
  - TB IGRA (if risk factors or CXR abnormal)
  - ANA, ANCA (if vasculitis suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **Chest X-ray (first-line)** – look for bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (sarcoidosis) or TB changes
  - Lower limb Doppler USS – if unilateral swelling to exclude DVT
- **Special tests:**
  - Skin biopsy (deep punch) – if atypical (e.g. ulceration, single nodule, trunk involvement) – shows septal panniculitis
  - Throat swab + rapid antigen test for Group A Strep
- **Never-miss test:** **Chest X-ray** — missing underlying sarcoidosis or TB can lead to delayed treatment and progressive lung disease
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess for sepsis — if febrile, tachycardic, or hypotensive → start sepsis protocol (blood cultures, IV fluids, antibiotics per local policy for streptococcal infection)
  - Analgesia — paracetamol ± NSAIDs if no contraindications
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Usually **do not admit** unless severe uncontrolled pain, extensive disease, systemic illness (sepsis), or high suspicion of TB/malignancy
  - If admitted: treat underlying cause (e.g. IV penicillin for Strep, stop culprit drug, start steroids for sarcoidosis)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Most EN self-resolves — simple analgesia, leg elevation, compression stockings for comfort
  - Severe / recurrent / idiopathic cases: consider colchicine, potassium iodide, or short-course prednisolone (under specialist guidance)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at maintenance rate if septic or unable to drink
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin if not anticoagulated and low bleeding risk
  - Diet: as tolerated
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2 if stable; more frequent if sepsis
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Paracetamol 1 g PO/IV QDS PRN (maximum 4 g/day)
  - Ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS (max 1200 mg/day) OR Naproxen 250–500 mg PO BD — use with food, avoid if renal impairment, asthma, peptic ulcer, or pregnancy
- **Common alternatives** (for severe/refractory EN):
  - Colchicine 500 μg PO BD — [check local policy] for dose adjustment in renal impairment
  - Prednisolone 20–40 mg PO OD for 5–7 days then taper — **must exclude active infection first**
  - Potassium iodide 300–600 mg PO TDS — [check local policy]; monitor thyroid function
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid in CrCl <30, active peptic ulcer, aspirin-sensitive asthma, pregnancy (third trimester)
  - Steroids: contraindicated in untreated TB, systemic fungal infections; use with caution in diabetes
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid in moderate-severe CKD; reduce dose in mild CKD ([check local policy])
  - Colchicine: reduce dose in renal impairment (e.g. eGFR <30 — [check local policy])
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Starting steroids without excluding infection** — can disseminate TB or worsen bacterial sepsis. Always treat underlying infection first.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Chronic or recurrent nodules (10–30% of cases)
  - Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation / scarring (rare)
  - Superimposed cellulitis (if nodules become excoriated)
- **Chronic:**
  - Progression of underlying disease (e.g. sarcoidosis leading to lung fibrosis; IBD causing bowel obstruction; TB dissemination)
sections.Prognosis:
- Excellent: 90% resolve completely in 3–6 weeks without treatment.
- Recurrence occurs in 10–20%, usually in relation to recurrent trigger (e.g. pregnancy, repeated Strep infections, oral contraceptive use).
- Long-term prognosis depends entirely on the underlying condition.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always get a CXR** in every patient with EN — it’s the single best test to detect sarcoidosis or TB.
- **Ask about recent drug history** (oral contraceptives, antibiotics, NSAIDs, sulphonamides, dapsone, hormones) — stopping the drug often resolves EN in 1–2 weeks.
- **Check a pregnancy test** in women of childbearing age — EN is common in pregnancy (especially late second trimester) and affects management (avoid NSAIDs).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Missing unilateral DVT** — never assume bilateral involvement without checking; a single swollen leg needs urgent USS.
- **Treating with steroids before sending stool cultures or a throat swab** — may mask an underlying infection and worsen outcome.
- **Assuming EN is idiopathic without investigation** — up to 50% have an identifiable cause; failure to find it may delay diagnosis of sarcoidosis, TB, or IBD.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** the nodules become ulcerated, spread rapidly, or if new systemic symptoms develop (fever, night sweats, cough, dyspnoea, blood in stool, joint swelling).
- **Return if:** severe pain not controlled by simple analgesia, or if leg becomes hot/swollen unilaterally (suggests DVT or cellulitis).
- **Follow-up:** review in 2–4 weeks with repeat CXR if initial was normal — if symptoms persist, refer to dermatology or rheumatology for biopsy.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Suspect necrotising fasciitis** (unilateral, crepitus, severe pain, systemic toxicity) — call senior immediately + surgical review; urgent CT or MRI.
- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** or **lactate > 2 mmol/L** — start sepsis 6 and involve medical registrar.
- **GCS < 15** or **suspected intracranial involvement** (e.g. in sarcoidosis with neuro-symptoms) — urgent neurology/senior review.
- **New hypoxia (SpO₂ < 94% on air)** or **chest pain with pleuritic features** — urgent CTPA if PE suspected; if CXR shows hilar lymphadenopathy, discuss respiratory team.

rawMarkdown:
## Erythema Nodosum
**One-liner:** Acute, tender, red nodular panniculitis on shins; usually reactive to infection, sarcoidosis, drugs, or IBD — rarely dangerous itself, but the trigger can be.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Necrotising fasciitis** (if unilateral or rapidly spreading, severe pain out of proportion, crepitus/bullae, systemic toxicity) — this is NOT erythema nodosum. If in doubt, call surgical registrar immediately.
- **Sepsis from underlying infection** (streptococcal pharyngitis, Yersinia, TB) — high fever, hypotension, raised lactate, focus on throat/lungs/gut.
- **Pulmonary embolism** (if unilateral leg swelling with pleuritic chest pain or hypoxia) — EN is bilateral; a single swollen leg should prompt DVT exclusion.

### Presentation
- Tender, erythematous, warm nodules: 1–5 cm diameter, typically bilateral on **anterior shins** (rarely thighs/arms)
- Nodules do **not** ulcerate, do **not** suppurate — they resolve over 3–6 weeks, leaving bruise-like discolouration
- Often preceded by:
  - Fever, malaise, arthralgia (ankles, knees)
  - Recent sore throat, cough, GI upset, or new medication
- **Key exam findings:** nodules are deep (panniculitis), non-mobile, exquisitely tender; overlying skin may be shiny; no ulceration; bilateral ± symmetrical

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Cellulitis / erysipelas** — unilateral, well-demarcated erythema, no discrete nodules; systemic upset out of proportion
- **Deep vein thrombosis** — unilateral swelling, calf tenderness, no nodules; Doppler USS needed
- **Vasculitis** (e.g. polyarteritis nodosa) — ulceration, livedo reticularis, multiorgan symptoms, palpable purpura
- **Insect bites** — asymmetrical, pruritic, central punctum
- **Pancreatic panniculitis** — nodules on legs + abdomen, occurs in acute pancreatitis (elevated lipase)
- **Lupus profundus** — more chronic, indurated plaques, associated SLE features
- **Splitter:** Bilateral shins → EN until proven otherwise; unilateral → rule out DVT/cellulitis first

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Temperature, heart rate, BP, respiratory rate, oxygen saturations
  - Throat inspection + palpation (exudate, cervical lymphadenopathy)
  - Examine chest (lymphadenopathy? sarcoidosis clue)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, ESR
  - Blood cultures (if febrile)
  - ASOT + anti-DNAse B (if suspected Group A Strep)
  - Stool cultures + Yersinia serology (if GI prodrome)
  - Serum ACE (sarcoidosis — not diagnostic alone but supportive)
  - TB IGRA (if risk factors or CXR abnormal)
  - ANA, ANCA (if vasculitis suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **Chest X-ray (first-line)** – look for bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (sarcoidosis) or TB changes
  - Lower limb Doppler USS – if unilateral swelling to exclude DVT
- **Special tests:**
  - Skin biopsy (deep punch) – if atypical (e.g. ulceration, single nodule, trunk involvement) – shows septal panniculitis
  - Throat swab + rapid antigen test for Group A Strep
- **Never-miss test:** **Chest X-ray** — missing underlying sarcoidosis or TB can lead to delayed treatment and progressive lung disease

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess for sepsis — if febrile, tachycardic, or hypotensive → start sepsis protocol (blood cultures, IV fluids, antibiotics per local policy for streptococcal infection)
  - Analgesia — paracetamol ± NSAIDs if no contraindications
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Usually **do not admit** unless severe uncontrolled pain, extensive disease, systemic illness (sepsis), or high suspicion of TB/malignancy
  - If admitted: treat underlying cause (e.g. IV penicillin for Strep, stop culprit drug, start steroids for sarcoidosis)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Most EN self-resolves — simple analgesia, leg elevation, compression stockings for comfort
  - Severe / recurrent / idiopathic cases: consider colchicine, potassium iodide, or short-course prednisolone (under specialist guidance)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at maintenance rate if septic or unable to drink
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin if not anticoagulated and low bleeding risk
  - Diet: as tolerated
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2 if stable; more frequent if sepsis

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Paracetamol 1 g PO/IV QDS PRN (maximum 4 g/day)
  - Ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS (max 1200 mg/day) OR Naproxen 250–500 mg PO BD — use with food, avoid if renal impairment, asthma, peptic ulcer, or pregnancy
- **Common alternatives** (for severe/refractory EN):
  - Colchicine 500 μg PO BD — [check local policy] for dose adjustment in renal impairment
  - Prednisolone 20–40 mg PO OD for 5–7 days then taper — **must exclude active infection first**
  - Potassium iodide 300–600 mg PO TDS — [check local policy]; monitor thyroid function
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid in CrCl <30, active peptic ulcer, aspirin-sensitive asthma, pregnancy (third trimester)
  - Steroids: contraindicated in untreated TB, systemic fungal infections; use with caution in diabetes
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid in moderate-severe CKD; reduce dose in mild CKD ([check local policy])
  - Colchicine: reduce dose in renal impairment (e.g. eGFR <30 — [check local policy])
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Starting steroids without excluding infection** — can disseminate TB or worsen bacterial sepsis. Always treat underlying infection first.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Chronic or recurrent nodules (10–30% of cases)
  - Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation / scarring (rare)
  - Superimposed cellulitis (if nodules become excoriated)
- **Chronic:**
  - Progression of underlying disease (e.g. sarcoidosis leading to lung fibrosis; IBD causing bowel obstruction; TB dissemination)

### Prognosis
- Excellent: 90% resolve completely in 3–6 weeks without treatment.
- Recurrence occurs in 10–20%, usually in relation to recurrent trigger (e.g. pregnancy, repeated Strep infections, oral contraceptive use).
- Long-term prognosis depends entirely on the underlying condition.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always get a CXR** in every patient with EN — it’s the single best test to detect sarcoidosis or TB.
- **Ask about recent drug history** (oral contraceptives, antibiotics, NSAIDs, sulphonamides, dapsone, hormones) — stopping the drug often resolves EN in 1–2 weeks.
- **Check a pregnancy test** in women of childbearing age — EN is common in pregnancy (especially late second trimester) and affects management (avoid NSAIDs).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Missing unilateral DVT** — never assume bilateral involvement without checking; a single swollen leg needs urgent USS.
- **Treating with steroids before sending stool cultures or a throat swab** — may mask an underlying infection and worsen outcome.
- **Assuming EN is idiopathic without investigation** — up to 50% have an identifiable cause; failure to find it may delay diagnosis of sarcoidosis, TB, or IBD.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** the nodules become ulcerated, spread rapidly, or if new systemic symptoms develop (fever, night sweats, cough, dyspnoea, blood in stool, joint swelling).
- **Return if:** severe pain not controlled by simple analgesia, or if leg becomes hot/swollen unilaterally (suggests DVT or cellulitis).
- **Follow-up:** review in 2–4 weeks with repeat CXR if initial was normal — if symptoms persist, refer to dermatology or rheumatology for biopsy.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Suspect necrotising fasciitis** (unilateral, crepitus, severe pain, systemic toxicity) — call senior immediately + surgical review; urgent CT or MRI.
- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** or **lactate > 2 mmol/L** — start sepsis 6 and involve medical registrar.
- **GCS < 15** or **suspected intracranial involvement** (e.g. in sarcoidosis with neuro-symptoms) — urgent neurology/senior review.
- **New hypoxia (SpO₂ < 94% on air)** or **chest pain with pleuritic features** — urgent CTPA if PE suspected; if CXR shows hilar lymphadenopathy, discuss respiratory team.
